Dictyostelium RacH Regulates Endocytic Vesicular
Trafficking and is Required for Localization of Vacuolin
Baggavalli P. Somesh1,2,†, Carola Neffgen1,†,
Miho Iijima3, Peter Devreotes3 and
Francisco Rivero1,*
1Center for Biochemistry and Center for Molecular
Medicine Cologne, Medical Faculty, University of
Cologne. Joseph-Stelzmann-Strasse 52, D-50931
Ko¨ln, Germany
2Current address: Cancer Research UK, Clare Hall
Laboratories, Blanche Lane, South Mimms, Herts EN6
3LD, London, UK
3Department of Cell Biology, Johns Hopkins University
School of Medicine, 725 North Wolfe Street, Baltimore,
MD 21205, USA
* Corresponding author: Francisco Rivero,
francisco.rivero@uni-koeln.de
Dictyostelium RacH localizes predominantly to mem-
branes of the nuclear envelope, endoplasmic recticulum
and Golgi apparatus. To investigate the role of this pro-
tein, we generated knockout and overexpressor strains.
RacH-deficient cells displayed 50% reduced fluid-phase
uptake and a moderate exocytosis defect, but phagocy-
tosis was unaffected. Detailed examination of the endo-
cytic pathway revealed defective acidification of early
endosomes and reduced secretion of acid phosphatase
in the presence of sucrose. The distribution of the post-
lysosomal marker vacuolin was altered, with a high pro-
portion of cells showing a diffuse vesicular pattern in
contrast to the wild-type strain, where few intensely
stained vacuoles predominate. Cytokinesis, cell motility,
chemotaxis and development appeared largely unaf-
fected. In a cell-free system, RacH stimulates actin poly-
merization, suggesting that this protein is involved in
actin-based trafficking of vesicular compartments. We
also investigated the determinants of subcellular localiza-
tion of RacH by expression of green-fluorescent-protein-
tagged chimeras in which the C-terminus of RacH and the
plasma-membrane-targeted RacG were exchanged, the
insert region was deleted or the net positive charge of
the hypervariable region was increased. We show that
several regions of the molecule, not only the hypervari-
able region, determine targeting of RacH. Overexpression
of mistargeted RacH mutants did not recapitulate the
phenotypes of a strain overexpressing nonmutated RacH,
indicating that the function of this protein is in great part
related to its subcellular localization.
Key words: actin, chemotaxis, cytokinesis, Dictyoste-
lium, endocytosis, vacuolin, hypervariable region, isopre-
nylation, Rho GTPase

publication 12 May 2006, published on-line 29 June 2006
Rho guanosine triphosphatases (GTPases) constitute
a major branch of the Ras superfamily of small GTPases.
They function as molecular switches that cycle between
an active GTP-bound form and an inactive GDP-bound
form. Cycling is tightly regulated by a large number of GTP
exchange factors (GEFs) and GTPase activating proteins
(GAPs) and by binding to GTP dissociation inhibitors
(GDIs). On activation, the Rho GTPases interact with
downstream target proteins to induce cellular responses.
Although primarily associated with cytoskeletal remodel-
ing, Rho GTPases have been shown to be also involved in
the regulation of a long list of cellular processes that in-
clude endocytosis, vesicle trafficking, morphogenesis,
cytokinesis, transcriptional activation, cell cycle progres-
sion and cellular proliferation (1). In fact, some members of
the Rho family have emerged as important regulators
of several stages of vesicle trafficking (2,3). The roles of
Rac1, Ccd42 and RhoA in the initial phase of endocytosis in
mammalian cells are well established, particularly during
phagocytosis and clathrin-independent internalization,
although there is also evidence for a regulatory role in
clathrin-mediated endocytosis. However, the actions of
Rho GTPases are not restricted to the plasma membrane.
Diverse studies point to a function of specific Rho GTPases
in endocytic trafficking pathways in intracellular compart-
ments. For example, RhoD regulates the motility of early
endosomes (4), whereas RhoB is involved in transport to
lysosomes (5). Cdc42 also functions at the Golgi appara-
tus, where it is recruited through interaction with the COPI
vesicle coat protein coatomer and triggers Arp2/3-dependent
actin assembly (6). Finally, several studies have put forward
the relevance of Rho GTPases in exocytosis. For example,
the Cdc42-related TC10 is required for translocation of Glut4
from storage vesicles to the plasma membrane (7).
In the model organism Dictyostelium discoideum, the Rho
family comprises 18 members (8–10). In addition to Rho
GTPases, other components of Rho-regulated signal trans-
duction pathways known in mammalian cells are also
present in this organism, like RhoGDI, the Arp2/3 complex,
PI 3-kinases, PAK, WASP, Scar/WAVE, formins and a large
number of GEFs and GAPs (9,11). This makes Dictyostelium
an attractive model to investigate the signaling events that
elicit remodeling of the cytoskeleton. While only few of the
Dictyostelium Rho GTPases have been studied to some
extent, the studies published to date give an idea of the
repertoire of cellular processes regulated by Rho GTPases

in this organism (see refs. 10 and 11 for reviews). RacB
and the three Rac1 proteins Rac1a, Rac1b and Rac1c have
been most extensively studied. They are involved in
chemotaxis, cell motility, endocytosis, cytokinesis and de-
velopment. RacC plays roles in actin cytoskeleton organi-
zation, pinocytosis, phagocytosis and cytokinesis. RacE
appears to be essential for cytokinesis and actin polymer-
ization but is not involved in phagocytosis. RacF1 localizes
to early phagosomes, macropinosomes and transient cell-
to-cell contacts, but inactivation of the racF1 gene does not
impair endocytosis and other actin-dependent processes,
probably due to the presence of a closely related RacF2. In
most cases, the exact signaling pathways regulated by
these proteins remain largely unknown.
Like Ras, Rho GTPases are synthesized as cytosolic
proteins but have the capacity to associate with mem-
branes by virtue of a series of posttranslational modifica-
tions of a C-terminal CAAX sequence motif (where C is
cysteine, A represents aliphatic amino acids and X is any
amino acid). This motif signals for covalent attachment of
an isoprenoid lipid unit to the cysteine residue. Two
distinct soluble CAAX prenyltransferases have been
described in eukaryotic cells: farnesyl transferase (FTase)
and geranylgeranyl transferase (GGTase). The FTase cata-
lyzes the addition of a C15 farnesyl unit to CAAX sequen-
ces where X is serine, methionine, cysteine, alanine or
glutamine. The GGTase catalyzes the addition of a C20
geranylgeranyl unit to CAAX sequences where X is leucine
or isoleucine. The prenyl-CAAX motif is substrate for
a specific protease that cleaves the AAX tripeptide. The
modified prenyl cysteine is then recognized by a specific
carboxyl methyl transferase that methylesterifies the a
carboxyl group. These two enzymes are membrane pro-
teins localized in the endoplasmic recticulum (ER) (12). The
CAAX motif alone targets proteins to the ER and Golgi
apparatus (13). For transport from the ER to the plasma
membrane, a second signal within the hypervariable region
upstream of the CAAX motif of Ras proteins has been
postulated, either palmitoylation (N-ras and H-ras) or
a polybasic amino acid stretch (K-ras4B) (14). In the case
of Rho GTPases, targeting is not only regulated by similar
determinants in the hypervariable region but also by
binding to RhoGDI and by additional factors that remain
to be elucidated (15). More recently, it was shown that the
type of lipid modification of the CAAX motif determines the
necessity of postprenylation processing: Ras, usually
farnesylated, requires further processing but not Rho,
usually geranylgeranylated (16).
Posttranslational modification by isoprenoid lipids is not
only essential for targeting, but it also appears to be critical
for GTPase function. This was initially investigated in Ras
proteins, where mutation of the CAAX motif or treatment
with FTase inhibitors abolishes plasma membrane associ-
ation and causes a complete loss of oncogenic transforming
activity (14). The functional relevance of protein prenylation
for Rho GTPases has been studied in RhoA and RhoB:
nonprenylated versions of activated RhoA or RhoB displayed
a loss of transforming activity (17,18), and the type of
prenylation in part determines the function of RhoB: farnesy-
lated RhoB is a growth promoting protein, whereas geranyl-
geranylated RhoB is an apoptosis-inducing protein (19,20).
In this study, we have undertaken the functional analysis of
Dictyostelium RacH. Although not a true Rac protein, RacH
is closer to Rac and Cdc42 (up to 74% similarity) than to
other subfamilies like Rho, Rif or Rnd (51–63% similarity)
(8,10). To investigate the role of this protein, we have
generated knockout cell lines and cell lines that overexpress
wild type (WT), constitutively active or dominant negative
RacH fused to green fluorescent protein (GFP). RacH is
targeted to intracellular membranes, predominantly of the
nuclear envelope, ER and Golgi apparatus. RacH appears to
be primarily involved in endosomal trafficking and has no
impact on cell motility or chemotaxis. The function of RacH is
in great part related to its subcellular localization, as revealed
by the functional analysis of mistargeted RacH mutants. In
a cell-free system, RacH stimulates actin polymerization,
suggesting that this protein might be involved in actin-based
trafficking of vesicular compartments.
Results
Knockout and overexpression of RacH
To investigate the function of RacH in vivo, we have
generated a knockout strain (RacH-KO) and stably trans-
formed cell lines that overexpress the WT and two
mutated variants of this protein fused to a GFP tag.
Mutation of Met-13 to Val (V13) renders the Rac protein
constitutively active, whereas mutation of Thr-18 to Asn
(N18) results in a dominant negative protein. The racH gene
was deleted by homologous recombination (Figure S1A),
and the recombination event was verified by Southern blot
(Figure S1B) and Western blot (Figure 1A). A polyclonal
antiserum generated against RacH recognized a protein of
approximately 25 kDa in total cell homogenates of AX2
that was absent in the knockout strain. Endogenous RacH
is sometimes visible as a double band, probably represent-
ing unprocessed and posttranslationally modified protein.
In cells overexpressing a GFP fussion of RacH-WT, the
antiserum recognized an additional protein of approximately
50 kDa that corresponds to the predicted size of GFP-RacH
(Figure 1A). The identity of this band was further confirmed
using a monoclonal antibody (mAb) against GFP (Figure 1B).
By comparison of the blots in Figure 1A,–B, we estimated
the levels of GFP-RacH-WT, V13 and N18 to be more than
10-fold higher than the amount of endogenous RacH.
Subcellular localization of RacH
The subcellular localization of RacH was studied by confo-
cal microscopy of vegetative cells overexpressing GFP-
RacH. Observations of live cells revealed a diffuse staining
all over the cell. However, after fixation, much of the RacH,
probably the cytosolic fraction, was washed off, allowing
the observation of the GFP fusion protein associated to

RacH in Dictyostelium
internal membrane compartments (Figure 2). An associa-
tion with the plasma membrane was observed in many
cases, but it was difficult to resolve from the fluorescence
signal of internal membranes close to the cell cortex (see
also Figure 11C). To identify the membranous compart-
ment(s) populated by RacH, we stained GFP-RacH cells
with antibodies against various marker proteins: the
nuclear envelope and ER marker interaptin, the Golgi
marker comitin, and the ER marker protein disulfide
isomerase (PDI). VatA, a subunit of the vacuolar proton
pump adenosine triphosphatase (ATPase), was used as
a marker for the contractile vacuole, where 90% of the
ATPase is located, and the endo/lysosomal system, where
the rest of the ATPase resides (21). Vacuolin was used as
a marker for a postlysosomal compartment, and an anti-
body against a common mannose-6-sulfate-containing
carbohydrate epitope was used as a lysosomal marker.
GFP-RacH clearly colocalized with interaptin and comitin
and to a great extent also with PDI. Colocalization with
VatA, vacuolin and lysosomes was apparent in some
instances but was not pronounced.
Morphology and actin distribution in RacH mutants
To examine the morphology and patterns of actin distribu-
tion of RacH mutants, we generated maximum projection
images from confocal sections through cells fixed and
stained with an actin-specific antibody (Figure 3). This pro-
cedure renders images with a pseudo-three-dimensional
aspect. In AX2 cells, actin displays a characteristic smooth
cortical staining, with enrichment at crown-like membrane
protrusions and short filopods. RacH-KO cells were hetero-
geneous in size, with a high proportion of cells of small
size, but there were no noticeable alterations in their pat-
tern of actin distribution. When grown in suspension,
RacH-WT cells were of irregular sizes (see also Figure S4),
and many, especially the bigger ones, did not have well-
developed crowns. RacH-V13 and N18 had a similar mor-
phology as RacH-WT when grown in suspension (not shown).
However, when grown on a substrate, these three mu-
tants were indistinguishable from AX2.
Growth and endocytosis of RacH mutants
The pattern of subcellular localization of RacH and the fact
that Rho GTPases participate in the regulation of endocytic
and exocytic pathways prompted us to examine in detail
vesicle trafficking processes in RacH mutants. We examined
the ability of RacH mutant cells to internalize fluorescently
labeled yeast particles (Figure 4A) or the fluid-phase marker
tetramethylrhodamine isothiocyanate-dextran (TRITC-
dextran) (Figure 4B). RacH-KO, V13 and N18 cells showed
normal rates of particle uptake. By contrast, RacH-WT
showed a severe defect, only reaching 30% of the AX2 rate.
RacH-KO cells and cells overexpressing RacH-V13 and
RacH-N18 were able to internalize the fluid-phase marker
TRITC-dextran to a lesser extent (approximately 50%) than
the control AX2 cells, but in cells overexpressing RacH-WT,
pinocytosis was almost abolished. The rates of pinocytosis
roughly correlated with the growth rates of these mutants
in suspension. RacH-WT cells had a severe growth defect:
they were able to grow only to a density slightly more than
2  106 cells/mL, with a very prolonged doubling time
(36.5 h). RacH-KO, V13 and N18 cells were slightly impaired:
they grew slowly (10 to 12 h versus 8.5 h for AX2) and
reached densities below that of AX2 cells (around 8  106
versus 10  106 cells/mL) (Figure S2). In the overexpressor
mutants, a cytokinesis defect (see below) is also likely
contributing to impaired growth in suspension. Finally, we
quantified exocytosis rates of TRITC-dextran and found that
RacH-KO cells showed a slower rate of exocytosis. RacH-
V13 and N18 cells were able to release the marker at the
same rate as AX2 cells, but RacH-WT cells were slightly
impaired (Figure 4C).
Acidification of early endosomes is impaired
in RacH-KO cells
As RacH seems to play a role in the regulation of
endocytosis, we further analyzed the endosomal and
vacuolar system of RacH-KO cells. First, we monitored
the change of endosomal pH during early phases of

pinocytosis and phagocytosis following a 10-min pulse of
fluorescein isothiocyanate-dextran (FITC-dextran) (Figure 5A)
or FITC-labeled bacteria (Figure 5B). In AX2 cells, endo-
somal acidification was rapid and reverted during the next
30 min. By contrast, in RacH-KO cells, endosomes became
on average less acidic. As endosomes are acidified by the
V(Hþ)-ATPase, we compared the levels of VatA, a compo-
nent of the peripheral catalytic domain of the vacuolar
proton pump, in RacH-KO cells (Figure 5C). The levels of
VatA did not differ between RacH-KO and AX2 cells.
Additionally, we visualized pH changes during endosomal
traffic in vivo. We exposed RacH-KO cells to medium
containing pH-sensitive FITC-dextran and pH-independent
TRITC-dextran. After 1 h, when cells have reached an
equilibrium of endocytosis and exocytosis, images of both
markers were collected (Figure 6A) and the distribution of
acidic and neutral vesicles was calculated. We did not
notice significant alterations: RacH-KO cells displayed
82.7% acidic and 17.3% neutral endosomes, and AX2
cells displayed 84.8% acidic and 15.2% neutral endo-
somes. Furthermore, we investigated the ability of endo-
somes to concentrate their contents by measuring the
intensity of TRITC-dextran in vesicles compared to its
concentration in the medium. The marker concentration
in acidic and neutral endosomes of AX2 and RacH-KO cells
did not differ significantly (Figure 6B).
Secretion of lysosomal enzymes
Like other lower eukaryotes, Dictyostelium cells partly
secrete lysosomal enzymes, likely due to incomplete
retrieval from the postlysosmal compartment (22). Mea-
suring the activity of lysosomal enzymes provides a means
to quantify the relative amounts of exocytosis versus
recycling of lysosomal enzymes. Some lysosomal en-
zymes, like acid phosphatase, enter endosomes early in
the pathway to profit from the acidic luminal pH value and
may be efficiently recycled and reused. Other enzymes,
like a-mannosidase, are delivered to endosomes later,
when their pH has returned to neutral, and tend to be
secreted at higher rates and thus accumulate in the
medium. Secretion of lysosomal enzymes can be stimu-
lated by nonmetabolizable sugars, like sucrose, that induce
endosomal swelling, thus counteracting membrane
retrieval processes (23). We compared the secretion of
acid phosphatase (Figure 7A) and a-mannosidase (Figure 7B)
in RacH-KO and AX2 cells. In the absence of sucrose,
lysosomal secretion of both enzymes was comparable in
RacH-KO and AX2 cells. Sucrose provoked an increase in
lysosomal enzyme secretion rates, especially acid phos-
phatase. In the case of a-mannosidase, no major differ-
ences were found between RacH-KO and AX2 cells; by
contrast, RacH-KO cells showed almost no increase of
secreted phosphatase activity.
Subcellular distribution of vacuolin is affected
in RacH-deficient cells
The pattern of subcellular localization of RacH and the
trafficking defects observed in RacH-KO cells led us to
investigate whether ablation of RacH would lead to alter-
ations of the pattern of distribution of any of the compart-
ments involved in transit of endocytic vesicles. In fixed cells,

antigen and vacuolin. Except for vacuolin, none of these
markers was noticeably altered in RacH-KO cells (Figure S3).
By contrast, the vacuolin distribution of RacH-KO cells was
remarkably distinct from the one of AX2 cells (Figure 8A).

Examination of the vacuolin distribution in overexpressor
mutants supported this observation: in cells overexpressing
RacH-WT and RacH-N18, the proportion of cells containing
large vacuoles was severely reduced, and most cells had
numerous middle-sized vacuoles. In RacH-V13 cells, the
proportion of cells with small, intensely stained vacuoles was
unusually large, and in many cases, vacuolin accumulated
strongly at the plasma membrane (Figure 8A,B).
RacH directly induces actin polymerization
We investigated the ability of RacH to induce actin
polymerization using a cell-free system (24). Under these
conditions, addition of guanosine triphosphate gamma S
(GTPgS) to lysates of RacH-V13 cells induced a higher actin
polymerization (about 150%) when compared to addition
of GTPgS to lysates of AX2 cells (about 120%). When the
cell lysate was incubated with a RacH-specific polyclonal
antiserum prior to addition of GTPgS, the response was
similar to that of an AX2 lysate. This demonstrates the
specificity of the response observed in RacH-V13 lysates
(Figure 9A). The ability of RacH to induce actin polymeri-
zation was confirmed in a rhodamine actin-based visual
assay. A trace amount of labeled actin was added to a cell
lysate to follow the behavior of endogenous actin. Using
a lysate of RacH-V13 cells, we observed that addition of
GTPgS stimulated actin polymerization around membrane
fragments containing GFP-RacH (Figure 9B).
Chemotaxis and cell motility
Aggregation competent cells move actively toward a cyclic
AMP (cAMP) gradient. Stimulation with cAMP elicits fast
and highly transient changes in the F-actin content that
correlate with changes in cell behavior (25). Rho-regulated

signaling pathways have been shown to be involved in this
process (11,26). It was therefore of interest to investigate
to what extent ablation or overexpression of RacH alters
actin polymerization and the motile behavior of aggrega-
tion competent cells. In AX2 cells, stimulation with cAMP
resulted in a rapid and transient 1.9-fold increase in the
amount of F-actin, followed immediately by a second much
lower peak that lasted until approximately 60 seconds. A
similar response was observed in RacH-KO and RacH-WT
cells (Figure 10A) as well as in RacH-V13 and RacH-N18
cells (not shown), although the second peak was abolished.
In a micropipette-based chemotaxis assay, AX2 and all
other mutant strains exhibited a similar rate of locomotion
(4–6 mm/min) in the absence of cAMP (Table 1). Parame-
ters like persistence, directionality and directional change
were indicative of random movement with frequent turns.
In the presence of cAMP, AX2 and RacH-V13 behaved
similarly, whereas RacH-KO, WT and N18 displayed a mod-
erately lower speed (8.0–9.6 versus 11.8 mm/min of AX2).
In general, RacH-KO cells performed moderately worse
than AX2 cells in the presence of cAMP. All these strains
became polarized, formed streams and migrated toward
the tip of the micropipette (Figure 10B).
Cytokinesis and development of RacH mutants
Rho-mediated signaling pathways have been shown to
participate in the regulation of cytokinesis in diverse
eukaryotic organisms (27). We were therefore interested
to investigate whether overexpression of RacH provoked
any alteration in this process. We stained cells grown
under shaking conditions or on a solid substrate with the
DNA-binding dye 40,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI)
(Figure S4). AX2 cells are usually mononucleate or binu-
cleate under any of these conditions. RacH-KO cells were
indistinguishable from AX2 cells. By contrast, in cultures of
RacH-WT cells grown under shaking conditions, 30% of
the cells had three or more nuclei. This defect was more
severe in RacH-V13 and N18 cells, where almost 60% of
the cells showed three or more nuclei [Figure S4A
(arrows),B]. Interestingly, the distribution of nuclei in
RacH-overexpressing strains grown on coverslips for
2 days did not differ from that in AX2 (not shown),
indicating that the cytokinesis defect of these strains is
conditional.
When starved on phosphate-buffered agar, RacH-KO cells
did not differ significantly from AX2 cells in the timing and
morphology of the developmental process (Figure S5).
Development studies in the overexpressor strains were
not attempted: whereas after 6 h of starvation, levels of
the GFP fusions were substantial, they became undetect-
able after 12 h, paralleling the downregulation of the actin-
15 promoter during development (28).
Determinants of RacH targeting
We sought to investigate the molecular determinants of
the pattern of subcellular localization exhibited by RacH. In
RacH, the last residue of the CAAX motif is glutamine
instead of methionine. A CAAQ motif is a substrate for
farnesylation, whereas a CAAM motif can be farnesylated
or geranylgeranylated (29). However, a RacH mutant in
which the last glutamine was exchanged by methionine
(Q200M) displayed the same subcellular localization as the
WT RacH (not shown). We then focused our attention on
the hypervariable region. We analyzed the C-terminal

sequence of RacH in comparison with Rho GTPases in
Dictyostelium that are targeted predominantly to the
plasma membrane, like RacF1 and RacG (Figure 11A). In
RacF1 and RacG, numerous lysine residues confer a net
charge of þ6 and þ8, respectively, to the hypervariable
region. By contrast, in RacH, interspersed acidic residues
lower the net charge to þ2. To test the hypothesis that
these charge differences are responsible for the differen-
tial localization of RacH and RacG, we made chimeric
constructs in which the C-terminal regions of RacH and
RacG were interchanged (Figure 11B). In confocal sec-
tions of fixed preparations, we noticed that RacH-chim
was enriched at the plasma membrane, resembling RacG.
RacG-chim distributed homogeneously throughout the cell
including the nucleus (Figure 11C). To rule out that the
strong plasma membrane association of RacG is due to
additional lipid modification of the cysteine residue 183
(Figure 11A, arrow), we exchanged this residue by serine.
This RacG C183S mutant behaved like the unmodified
RacG, indicating that net positive charge is sufficient for
plasma membrane targeting of this molecule. The charge
effect was further investigated in alanine-exchange con-
structs. Here, aspartic acid residues of the hypervariable
region of RacH were exchanged by alanine residues in
order to increase the net positive charge of this region
(Figure 11A, asterisks). Interestingly, both RacH-4Ala and
RacG-chim-4Ala constructs displayed increased, but not
predominant, accumulation at the plasma membrane
(Figure 11C). A similar behavior was observed in a RacH-
2Ala mutant (not shown). This indicates that net charge of
the hypervariable region is important but is not the only
determinant for proper targeting of RacH, suggesting the
presence of an additional targeting signal within the C-
terminal region of RacH.

region is characteristic of Rho proteins and required for
activation of some effectors but not others. It is usually

The pattern of membrane association of these mutants
was further studied by differential centrifugation of ly-
sates, followed by Western blot analysis of the resultant
cytosolic and particulate fractions (Figure 11B). In the case
of RacH, approximately 60% of the protein was cytosolic
and 40% membrane associated. RacG was predominantly
membrane associated, 70% being present in the particu-
late fraction. These proportions became reversed in the
chimeric mutants. The alanine-exchange mutants dis-
played an increased proportion of membrane-associated
protein, particularly the RacH-4Ala mutant, whereas the
RacHDins mutant appeared equally distributed between
the cytosolic and the membrane fractions.
Proper targeting of RacH is required for function
Because exchange of the C-terminal region leads to
mislocalization of the protein, we sought to investigate
how overexpression of a mislocalized protein affects
diverse Rho-regulated processes like morphology, endo-
cytosis, cytokinesis, motility and chemotaxis and how this
compares to overexpression of the properly targeted pro-
tein. To this end, we analyzed the phenotype of strains
overexpressing the GFP-tagged mistargeted mutants
RacH-chim, RacG-chim, RacHDins and RacH-TM. The last
was targeted to the mitochondrial outer membrane by in-
frame fusion to the transmembrane domain of Miro (30)
and was predominantly membrane associated (Figures 11
B,C, S6). With the exception of RacH-chim, these con-
structs expressed at levels comparable to RacH-N18
(Figure 1B). Although RacH-chim expressed at low levels,
this was sufficient to elicit a phenotype.
RacH-chim cells displayed abundant actin-filled long filo-
pods with occasional branching. In live cells, filopods were
frequently very long and highly motile and emanated from
lamellipods (Figure 3, phase contrast images). These cells
very closely resembled RacG overexpressors (our unpub-
lished results). RacG-chim cells displayed a morphology
and pattern of actin distribution similar to AX2 cells. None
of the mistargeted constructs showed the strongly
impaired growth and pinocytosis defects of RacH-WT
(Figures 4B, S2), and only in RacG-chim was phagocytosis
severely impaired (Figure 4A). The cytokinesis defect
apparent in cells overexpressing RacH-WT growing in
suspension was no longer appreciable in cells overex-
pressing RacG-chim, RacH-chim or RacHDins (Figure S4).
RacH-TM cells, by contrast, were slightly impaired, with
30% cells with three or more nuclei, and this defect was
no longer observable in cells grown on a solid substrate.
Finally, RacH-chim and RacG-chim cells showed a lower
increase in the first peak of actin polymerization upon
cAMP stimulation, but the second peak was still apprecia-
ble (Figure 10A). However, none of these two strains was
severely impaired in its ability to migrate toward the source
of chemo-attractant (Figure 10B and Table 1). All this
indicates that the phenotype elicited by overexpression
of RacH is strongly dependent of its subcellular localiza-
tion. To further substantiate this hypothesis, we investi-
gated whether re-expression of GFP-tagged mistargeted
RacH mutants was able to revert the pinocytosis defect
observed in the RacH-KO cells. However, this approach
yielded inconsistent results because our attempts resulted
in strains that expressed the fusion protein at levels well
above those of the endogenous RacH and therefore
displayed an overexpressor phenotype (not shown).

Discussion
RacH constitutes the first reported Dictyostelium Rho
GTPase specifically retained at internal membrane com-
partments, where it appears to regulate vesicle trafficking
processes. The localization of RacH at membranes of the
ER and Golgi apparatus along with the defects observed in
a RacH-deficient strain are compatible with the proposed
role in trafficking events along the endocytic pathway.
Although not specifically investigated, indirect evidence
suggests that the secretory pathway is unaffected: che-
motaxis and development, two processes that depend on
maturation and sorting of membrane receptors and adhe-
sion molecules, were not affected in the mutants studied
here.
In Dictyostelium, the endocytic pathway has been opera-
tionally divided into four steps: uptake at the plasma
membrane, transit through early acidic endosomes, fol-
lowed by a late neutral compartment and finally exocytosis
(31). We show that RacH-KO cells have defects in all four
steps. Although a fraction of RacH appears to localize at
the plasma membrane, we attribute the 50% decrease in
fluid-phase uptake and the moderate exocytosis defect of
the mutant to alterations in trafficking away from the
plasma membrane rather than to deficient signaling at this
location. This is supported by the fact that targeting of
RacH to the plasma membrane (RacH-chim mutant), while
stimulating the formation of filopods, does not elicit any
alteration in fluid-phase uptake or release. This is in
contrast to Rho GTPases that regulate the initial phase of
endocytosis by acting at the plasma membrane, like the
well-established Rac1, Cdc42 and RhoA in mammalian
cells (2,3) and presumably RacF1, Rac1, RacC and RacG in
Dictyostelium (32–35 and our unpublished observations).
It might seem paradoxical that overexpression of both
constitutively active and dominant negative RacH results in
a similar phenotype, namely impaired pinocytosis. An
inhibitory effect of the same biological function by opposite
mutants has already been described. To mention only one
example, Dictyostelium cells expressing constitutively
active or dominant negative Rac1 display inefficient che-
motaxis toward cAMP (36). This effect can be interpreted
by taking into account how those mutants work in cells.
Whereas RacH-N18 might be acting by competing with
other Rho GTPases for binding to RhoGEFs, RacH-V13
might be saturating the pool of effectors. Taking into
account that overexpression levels are very high, results
from RacH-WT, V13 and N18 might not always reflect the
physiological role of RacH and must therefore be inter-
preted very cautiously. Note for example that all three
overexpressors bring about a cytokinesis defect, but no
such defect is apparent in the knockout strain.

RacH-KO cells also showed deficient acidification of early
endosomes, a process driven by accumulation of vacuolar
Hþ-ATPase. Clarke et al. (37) have shown that in Dictyo-
stelium, most of the proton pumps delivered to a new
endosome are retrieved from late endosomes. Concentra-
tion of fluid-phase marker in postlysosomal compartments
is not altered in RacH-KO, suggesting that retrograde
transport of the pump is not affected. Taken together,
our data pinpoint the role, or one of the roles, of racH to the
delivery of vesicles carrying proton pumps to early endo-
somes. If RacH is involved specifically in this step, it is not
surprising that neither the protein levels nor the distribu-
tion of VatA, a component of the vacuolar proton pump,
was noticeably altered in RacH-KO because most of the
proton pump is localized on vesicles of the contractile
vacuole system, a compartment that apparently does not
exchange membranes with the endolysosomal compart-
ment (37). The lower amounts of secreted acid phospha-
tase observed in RacH-KO cells in the presence of sucrose
might be related to a deficient acidification of endosomes:
previous reports on Acanthamoeba indicated that hydro-
lase transport and secretion is pH dependent (38).

While vacuolin A is dispensable, disruption of vacuolin B
results in markedly impaired exocytosis, enlargement of
the neutral postlysosomal compartment and accumulation
of the fluid-phase marker (40). Except for a moderate
exocytosis defect, none of these defects was observed
in RacH-KO cells, suggesting that in spite of its altered
distribution, function of vacuolin is not affected. The pre-
dominantly diffuse pattern of vacuolin distribution seen in
RacH-KO suggests that RacH is necessary for accumula-
tion of vacuolin at larger vesicles prior to exocytosis.
Targeting of vacuolin has not been studied in detail, but
experiments using latrunculin A or a hybrid protein that
targets cofilin to the postlysosomal compartment have
revealed a role of the F-actin cytoskeleton in this process
(41). It is interesting to note that other proteins of the PHB
domain family like stomatin and podocin also colocalize
partially with the actin cytoskeleton (39).
The mechanism of RacH action is not clear at the moment.
LikeCdc42,RacHcouldbeinvolvedinvesicletransportwithin
and from the Golgi apparatus by promoting actin-driven
vesicle movement (42). RacH is able to induce actin polymer-
ization, and it is well established that progression of vesicles
along the endocytic pathway depends on actin (40,41).
However, RacH does not interact with WASP or any other
effector carrying a CRIB domain (26,43 and our unpublished
observations). RacH does also not interact with IQGAP-
related proteins (our unpublished observations), which have
been reported to associate with Cdc42 at Golgi membranes
(44). In mammalian cells, RhoD regulates motility of early
endosomes through interaction with a Diaphanous-related
formin (4); therefore, formins may constitute a candidate
family of RacH effectors to be addressed in future studies.

RacH in Dictyostelium
In this study, we also analyzed the importance of the CAAX
motif and the second signal in the hypervariable region for
targeting and functionality of Dictyostelium RacH. RacH is
predominantly cytosolic, but 40% of the protein is targeted
to the perinuclear region, Golgi apparatus and ER, closely
resembling the behavior of human Cdc42 and Rac2 (15).
We initially attributed this localization of RacH to lack of
a functional second signal: negatively charged residues in
this region lower the net positive charge to þ2 (Figure 11A).
It has been shown that a net positive charge of 4 or more is
required to target K-ras4B to the plasma membrane
(14,45), and indeed, human Cdc42 and Rac2, which have
a weak polybasic region, are targeted predominantly to
internal membranes (15). By contrast, in Dictyostelium
Rho GTPases that are targeted to the plasma membrane,
like Rac1a/1b/1c (33), RacC (46), RacE (47), RacF1 (32) and
RacG, the hypervariable region has charges that range
from þ4 to þ8, similar to that in human Rac1 (15). Chimeric
constructs of RacG and RacH in which the C-terminal
region was exchanged appeared to confirm the role of the
net positive charge of the hypervariable region: the RacH
chimeric construct was targeted to the plasma membrane,
whereas the RacG chimeric construct was retained at
endomembranes. This is in-line with the results obtained
with human Cdc42 and TC10 (a Cdc42-related GTPase).
TC10 possesses both a palmitoylation signal and a poly-
basic stretch and localizes on the plasma membrane and
endosomes. A chimeric construct in which the C-terminal
20 amino acids were replaced by the homologous region of
Cdc42 displayed a localization identical to Cdc42 (15).
These and other results reported by Michaelson et al.
(15) led to propose that subcellular localization of Rho
GTPases is determined primarily by the hypervariable
region and the capacity to bind RhoGDI. A role for RhoGDI
in the localization of RacG or RacH, however, can be
excluded because neither of both proteins interacts with
RhoGDI (48). A more detailed analysis using alanine-
exchange mutants revealed that further sequences
upstream of the polybasic region within the 38 residues
exchanged in the chimeric mutants contribute to targeting
of RacH: the RacH-4Ala and the RacG-chim-4Ala mutants,
although carrying a net positive charge of þ6, were still
substantially retained at endomembranes.
One more determinant of the subcellular localization of RacH
appears to be the insert region. Although this region does
not change conformation upon activation, in combination
with the effector domain, it contributes to determine the
specificity of interactions of Rho GTPases (49). For example,
although not required for binding to, it is required for
activation of the nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phos-
phate, reduced form, (NADPH) oxidase complex by Rac (50)
or phospholipase D1 by Cdc42 (51). Altogether, our results
indicate that regions other than the hypervariable region are
responsible for proper targeting of Rho GTPases, and this
might be a more general principle applicable to lipid-modified
GTPases of the Ras superfamily, as has been shown
recently for Rab GTPases in an extensive study (52).
The fact that RacG and RacH display radically different
subcellular localizations and play different roles was ex-
ploited to investigate to which extent targeting of the
GTPase to a particular region or compartment determines
its biological functions. RacG regulates mainly processes
that require actin remodeling at the cell cortex: over-
expression of RacG elicits extension of filopods, stimu-
lates particle uptake and impairs chemotaxis (our
unpublished observations). Targeting of RacH to the

plasma membrane was accompanied by extension of
filopods and impaired F-actin response to cAMP but fairly
normal chemotactic response. By contrast, targeting of
RacG to internal membranes impaired phagocytosis but
had no effect on the rate of growth and pinocytosis or on
cytokinesis, which are severely affected in RacH-WT cells.
This indicates that RacH is able to interact with effectors
and/or regulatory elements of other Rho GTPases at the
plasma membrane but is prevented of doing so by
retention at inner membrane compartments. The conclu-
sion that targeting to the appropriate membrane compart-
ment is necessary for the Rho GTPase to elicit its functions
was further supported by results from RacHDins and
RacH-TM mutants: overexpression of these mutated RacH
proteins did not recapitulate any of the phenotypes elicited
by overexpression of RacH-WT (the moderate cytokinesis
defect of RacH-TM might result from alterations of the
mitochondrial function). Reports that address the impor-
tance of membrane targeting of Rab GTPases also under-
score our conclusion. Rab27a, a protein that associates
specifically with melanosomes, did not rescue the coat
color phenotype of a Rab27a null mouse when mistargeted
to the ER (53), and in yeast, mistargeted lipid tail variants of
two essential Rab proteins, Ypt1p and Sec4p, were not
able to complement thermosensitive alleles of the respec-
tive gene (54). The fact that RacH-TM does not induce
accumulation of actin around mitochondria (Figure S6)
favor the explanation that the effects elicited by RacH
depend on the interaction with regulators and effectors
restricted to specific locations within the cell.
In summary, we show that RacH is involved in regulation
of vesicle trafficking, more specifically in sorting of vesicles
from the secretory pathway to several compartments
along the endocytic pathway, presumably by virtue of its
effects on actin polymerization. We also show that several
regions of the molecule, not only the hypervariable region,
determine targeting of RacH and that targeting to the
correct membrane compartment probably allows interac-
tions with appropriate regulators and effectors and is
therefore essential for function.
Materials and Methods
Strains, growth conditions, and development
of D. discoideum
Cells of D. discoideum strain AX2 (referred to as WT) and transformants
were grown either in liquid nutrient medium at 218C, with shaking at
160 r.p.m. (55), or on nutrient agar plates with Klebsiella aerogenes (56).
For development, the cells were grown to a density of 2  106 to 3 
106 cells/mL, washed in 17-mM Soerensen phosphate buffer of pH 6.0, and
0.5  108 cells were deposited on phosphate agar plates and allowed
to develop by incubating at 218C as described (57).
Generation of a racH knockout strain
To construct the racH disruption vector, DNA fragments containing the 5‘
untranslated region (UTR) of racH were amplified from genomic DNA using
primers PracH: 837 (5’-GTCGACATAGTAATAGCAACAATCATAATGG-3‘),
and PracH: 27 (5’-AAGCTTAACCATTACTTTAATATCTTTTACC-3‘), and di-
gested with SalI and HindIII. DNA fragments containing the 3’UTR region of
racH were amplified using primers PracH: 1885 (5‘-TCTAGAAAAAAAGGT-
GATAAAGACTCAAAGG-3’), and PracH: þ910 (5‘-GCGGCCGCACCAAG-
TAATGATAGTATAGAATCC-3’), and digested with XbaI and NotI.
Blasticidin-S-resistance (Bsr) cassette (58) was excissed from pLPBLPv2
(M. Landree, Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, unpublished data) using
HindIII and XbaI. These three DNA fragments were cloned into pBluescript
(Stratagene, La Jolla, CA, USA), forming the disruption vector pRacHdis
(Figure S1A). After digestion with SalI and NotI, linearized pRacHdis (10 mg)
was introduced into growth phase of AX2 cells by electroporation. Trans-
formants were selected in nutrient medium containing 5 mg/mL blasticidin
(ICN Biomedicals Inc., Aurora, OH, USA) on plastic dishes. After 5 days, the
cells were harvested and cloned on SM agar plates with K. aerogenes.
Resultant colonies were checked for disruption of the racH gene by
polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and Southern blot analysis (Figure S1B).
Mutagenesis of RacH
Dictyostelium RacH sequences carrying M13V (constitutively active) or T18N
(dominant negative) mutations were generated from WT complementary
DNA (cDNA) by PCR-based, site-directed mutagenesis (26,43). PCR-based
mutagenesis with mismatched reverse primers was used to generate
a Q200M mutant of RacH, a C183S mutant of RacG, and mutants in which
aspartic acid residues were substituted by alanine residues (Figure 11A).
Chimeric PCR products were generated by two PCR steps. In the first step,
N-terminal fragments of RacG (amino acids 1–168) and RacH (amino acids
1–163) and C-terminal fragments of RacG (amino acids 167–201) and RacH
(amino acids 164–200) were amplified (Figure 11A). The oligonucleotide
primers were designed to allow an overlap of 15 bases between the N-
terminal fragment of one GTPase and the C-terminal fragment of the other.
Finally, the full-length chimeric cDNAs were amplified using a RacG forward
primer and a RacH reverse primer to obtain RacG chimera and a RacH
forward primer and a RacG reverse primer to obtain RacH chimera. To
generate a RacH lacking the Rho-insert region (residues 125-131;
RacHDins), overlaping fragments upstream and downstream of the insert
were amplified separately by PCR on a RacH template and joined in
a second PCR step. A mitochondrial-membrane-targeted RacH was gener-
ated by fusing in-frame RacH to the C-terminal transmembrane region
(amino acids 603–658) of Dictyostelium Miro using conventional cloning of
PCR-amplified fragments.
Polymerase chain reaction products were cloned into the pGEM-Teasy vector
(Promega, Madison, WI, USA) and verified by sequencing, done at the service
laboratory of the Center for Molecular Medicine, Cologne, using an automated
sequencer (ABI 377 PRISM; Perkin Elmer, Norwalk, CO, USA).
Vectors were constructed that allowed expression of the red-shifted S65T
mutant of Aequoria victoria GFP fused to the amino terminus of RacH in
Dictyostelium cells. Because the carboxyl terminus of Rho-related proteins
contains structural elements responsible for membrane association (45),
a fusion of GFP at the amino-terminal end of RacH was chosen. For
overexpression of mutated variants V13 and N18, a tetracycline-controlled
inducible system was used (59). Fusions to GFP were prepared in pBlue-
script (Stratagene) and blunt end cloned into the MB38 plasmid. This vector
was introduced into AX2 cells carrying the MB35 vector. For overexpres-
sion of the WT form of RacH and all other mutants, cDNA fragments were
cloned in the transformation vector pDEX-GFP under the control of the
actin-15 promoter (60). This vector confers G418 resistance and was
introduced into AX2 cells. All vectors were introduced into cells by electro-
poration. G418 (Sigma, Deisenhofen, Germany) and blasticidin were used
for selection. GFP-expressing transformants were confirmed by visual
inspection under a fluorescence microscope.
Antibody generation
A PCR-amplified cDNA fragment encoding RacH was cloned into the
BamHI site of the bacterial protein expression vector pQE30 (Qiagen
GmbH, Hilden, Germany). This vector was transformed into Escherichia

was purified using affinity chromatography with Ni2þ-NTA agarose beads,
and 100 mg of protein was used to immunize two female, white New
Zealand rabbits (Pineda Antiko¨rper-Service, Berlin, Germany), followed by
two boosts of 100 mg each at 2-week intervals. Specificity of the antiserum
was verified against a panel of glutathione S-transferase fusions of
Dictyostelium Rho GTPases.
Western blotting
The SDS-PAGE and Western blotting were performed using standard
methods. An enhanced chemiluminescence detection system (Amersham
Pharmacia Biotech, Freiburg, Germany) was used. GFP was detected with
specific mAb K3-184-2, actin with mAb Act 1-7 (61), and a-actinin with mAb
47-62-17 (62).
Cell fractionation experiments
GFP-RacH cells were collected by centrifugation (1000 g for 5 min) and
resuspended at a density of 2  108 cells/mL in MES buffer (20 mM 2-[N-
morpholino] ethane sulfonic acid, 1 mM ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid,
250 mM sucrose, pH 6.5) supplemented with a protease inhibitor mixture
(Roche Diagnostics, Mannheim, Germany). Cells were homogenized on ice
by sonication and light microscopy was performed to ensure that at least
95% of the cells were broken. Cytosolic and particulate fractions were
separated by ultracentrifugation (100 000g for 30 min).
Conventional and fluorescence microscopy
To record distribution of GFP fusion proteins in living cells, cells were grown
to a density of 2  106 to 3  106 cells/mL and transferred onto 18-mm
glass coverslips with a plastic ring for observation. For studies on fixed
cells, cells were fixed either in cold methanol (208C) or at room
temperature with picric acid/paraformaldehyde (15% v/v of a saturated
aqueous solution of picric acid/2% paraformaldehyde, pH 6.0), followed by
70% ethanol. Actin was detected using mAb Act 1-7 (61), interaptin using
mAb 260-60-10 (63), PDI using mAb 221-135-1 (64), comitin using mAb
190-340-2 (65), the A subunit of the V/Hþ-ATPase using mAb 221-35-2 (40),
a common mannose-6-sulfate-containing carbohydrate epitope present in
lysosomal enzymes using mAb 221-342-5 (66), and vacuolin using mAb
221-1-1 (67), followed by incubation with Cy3-labeled anti-mouse immuno-
globulin G. Nuclei were stained with DAPI (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO,
USA). Mitochondria were stained with MitoTracker Red CMXRos (Molec-
ular Probes Europe, Leiden, Netherlands) for 45 min prior to fixation.
Confocal images were taken with an inverted Leica TCS-SP laser scanning
microscope with a 100 HCX PL APO NA 1.40 oil immersion objective
(Leica Microsystems, Wetzlar, Germany). For excitation, the 488-nm argon-
ion laser line and the 543-nm NeNe laser line were used. Images were
processed using the accompanying software.
Actin polymerization assay
Chemo-attractant-induced F-actin formation in aggregation competent cells
was quantitated as described (25). Briefly, cells were resuspended at
2  107 cells/mL in Soerensen buffer and starved for 6–8 h. Cells were
stimulated with 1 mM cAMP and 50 mL of samples were taken at various
time points. The reaction was terminated by addition of 450 mL stop
solution (3.7% formaldehyde, 0.1% Triton-X-100 (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis,
MO, USA), 0.25 mM TRITC-phalloidin in 20 mM potassium phosphate,
10 mM Pipes, 5 mM EGTA, 2 mM MgCl2, pH 6.8). After staining for 1 h,
samples were centrifuged for 5 min at 15 000g. Pellets were extracted
using 1 mL methanol for 16 h, and fluorescence (540/565 nm) was read in
a PTI fluorimeter (Photon Technology Intl., Seefeld, Germany).
For quantification of actin polymerization in a cell-free system, lysates were
prepared as described (24). Briefly, cells were starved as described above,
resuspended at 3  108 cells/mL in cold PM (10 mM phosphate buffer of
pH 6.1, 2 mM MgSO4, 20 mM KCl, 1 mM EGTA) and lysed on ice by
sonication. Actin polymerization was induced by addition of 100 mM GTPgS.
F-actin was quantitated by TRITC-phalloidin staining of formaldehyde-
stabilized pelleted material as indicated above, with the difference that
0.4 mM TRITC-phalloidin was contained in the stop solution.
For visualization of actin polymerization, a modification of the method of Ma
et al. (68) was used. Cells were resuspended at 1.5  108 cells/mL in lysis
buffer (10 mM Hepes, pH 7.7, 100 mM KCl, 2 mM MgCl2, 0.1 mM CaCl2,
5 mM EGTA, 50 mM sucrose, 1 mM DTT and protease inhibitor mixture) and
lysed by sonication. About 7.5 mL of cell extract was mixed with 1 mL of
rhodamine-labeled rabbit muscle actin (0.3 mg/mL; Cytoskeleton, Denver,
CO, USA) and 0.5 mL of ATP regeneration mix (150 mM creatine phosphate,
20 mM ATP, 2 mM EGTA, 25 mM MgCl2). After addition of 1 mL GTPgS (final
concentration of 1 mM), 5 mL of the reaction mixture was deposited on a
coverslip and squashed with a slide for microscopy.
Chemotaxis assay
Vegetative cells were resuspended at 1  107 cells/mL in Soerensen
phosphate buffer and starved for 6–8 h. Cells were then transferred onto
a 5-cm glass coverslip with a plastic ring placed on an Olympus IX70 inverse
microscope equipped with a 10 UplanFl 0.3 objective (Olympus, Hamburg,
Germany). Cells were stimulated with a glass capillary micropipette
(Eppendorf Femtotip) filled with 0.1 M cAMP (69). Time-lapse image series
were captured and stored on a computer hard drive at 30-second intervals
with a JAI CV-M10 CCD camera and a Imagenation PX610 frame grabber
(Imagenation Corp., Beaverton, OR, USA) controlled through Optimas
software (Optimas Corp., Bothell, Washington). The DIAS software (Soltech,
Oakdale, IA, USA) was used to trace individual cells along image series and
calculate the cell motility parameters (70).
Endocytosis
Phagocytosis was assayed as described using TRITC-labeled yeast cells
(71). Fluid-phase endocytosis and exocytosis assays were performed using
TRITC-dextran or FITC-dextran according to Aubry et al. (72).
Secretion of lysosomal enzymes
Cells were harvested by centrifugation and resuspended at a concentration of
1  107 cells/mL in Soerensen buffer with or without addition of sucrose
(100 mM). One-milliliter samples was harvested by centrifugation at the times
indicated. Enzyme assays were performed on the supernatant (extracellular)
and on cells lysed in the presence of 0.1% Triton-X-100. The acid phosphatase
and a-mannosidase activities were measured as described (22).
Determination of endolysosomal pH
The endosomal pH was measured by a dual excitation ratio method
(excitation at 450 and 495 nm and emission at 520 nm) using FITC-dextran
(70 000 Mr; Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) (2 mg/mL) or FITC-labeled
E. coli B/r as pH probes as described by Temesvari et al. (21). Briefly, the
cells were grown to a density of 2  105 to 5  105 cells/mL, harvested
and resuspended at a concentration of 3  106 cells/mL in fresh axenic
medium, and loaded with the pH probe. For measurement, the cells were
collected by centrifugation, washed and resuspended in 50 mM MES
buffer, pH 6.5, and the fluorescence intensity was measured using a PTI
fluorimeter. To monitor changes of endosomal pH during transit of the fluid
or solid-phase markers, the cells were pulsed with either FITC-dextran or
FITC-labeled bacteria for 10 min, washed and resuspended in fresh nutrient
medium. The pH was determined from an in vitro standard curve of FITC-
dextran in a pH range of 4–7.
To monitor changes of pH and concentration of marker during endocytosis
(40), vegetative cells were washed with Soerensen buffer and allowed to
sit in an observation chamber made of a glass coverslip with a plastic ring.
Cells were then exposed to pH-sensitive FITC- and pH-independent TRITC-
dextran for 1 h, and single confocal sections were taken as indicated above.
Miscellaneous methods
Standard molecular biology methods were applied as described by
Sambrook and Russell (73).
